questions.question: What percentage did random seating proximity increase friendship probability in the large-scale classroom experiment?

options.text: From 5% to 10%
From 15% to 22%
From 25% to 50%
From 50% to 75%

questions.explanation: The randomized experiment (182 classrooms, N=2,966 students) showed that random seating proximity increased mutual friendship probability from 15% to 22% — a 47% relative increase. This demonstrates that physical nearness is a stronger predictor of friendship than personality compatibility or shared interests. The practical implication: engineering proximity (joining recurring activities where you see the same people) is the highest-leverage intervention for adult friendship formation.

questions.question: According to the Aron et al. self-disclosure paradigm, what accelerates interpersonal closeness?

options.text: Staying at surface-level small talk indefinitely
Immediately sharing very deep personal information with strangers
Structured, gradually escalating reciprocal self-disclosure
Avoiding any personal topics and only discussing facts

questions.explanation: The Aron et al. study (famous '36 questions') demonstrated that structured escalating disclosure significantly accelerates closeness. The key principles: gradual depth (Level 1 → 2 → 3 → 4), reciprocity (both people share), and vulnerability matching (don't overshare relative to the other person's comfort). This isn't manipulation — it's creating conditions for genuine connection by intentionally building trust through progressive sharing.

questions.question: Why are repeated unplanned interactions more effective for friendship formation than one-time high-stakes events?

options.text: They're not — one-time networking events are more effective
Low-cost investment per interaction, reduced performance pressure, cumulative disclosure over time
Unplanned interactions guarantee instant chemistry
High-stakes events are always better for building trust

questions.explanation: Repeated unplanned interactions work because: (1) each interaction requires minimal emotional investment (low-stakes), (2) performance pressure is reduced ('this is just a casual run-in'), (3) small conversations accumulate into deeper knowledge over time, and (4) familiarity reduces social threat. One-time high-pressure events create anxiety and rarely lead to follow-through. Consistent showing up beats charismatic one-time performances.

questions.question: What is the evidence grade for the proximity effect in adult friendship formation?

options.text: STRONG — multiple RCTs demonstrate causal effect
MODERATE — proximity effect highly replicated, but adult friendship RCT equivalent unavailable
WEAK — only anecdotal evidence exists
LIMITED — contradicted by most research

questions.explanation: Evidence grade is MODERATE. The proximity effect is one of the most replicated findings in social psychology (dorm neighbors, workplace proximity, classroom seating), BUT there are no large RCTs testing 'adult friendship formation interventions' in general populations. The principles are evidence-informed, not evidence-proven at the level of clinical trials. This doesn't mean they don't work — it means we're applying well-established principles to a context that hasn't been experimentally tested in adults.

questions.question: When is a life transition window LEAST useful for friendship formation?

options.text: When you've just moved to a new city
When you've just started a new job
When you've been in the same stable environment for 5+ years with established social circles
When you've just become a parent and are meeting other new parents

questions.explanation: Life transitions (new city, new job, new school, becoming a parent) create open social networks where everyone is seeking new connections. Established environments have solidified social circles that are harder to break into. This doesn't mean it's impossible to make friends in stable environments — but the research shows transitions create windows of opportunity when friendship formation is structurally easier. Take advantage of transition windows by actively seeking connection early.

title: The Science of Friendship Formation - Quiz
